activities.title: Money in, money out
activities.content:
A **budget** is a plan for your money. It tells you how much money is coming in (income) and how much is going out (expenses) over a set period — usually a week or month.

Without a budget, most people spend first and wonder later why they have nothing left. A budget flips this — you decide in advance where every dollar goes.
activities.notePrompt: In your own words, what is the main purpose of a budget?

activities.title: Why most Kiwis don't budget
activities.content:
Research shows only about **1 in 3 New Zealanders** regularly tracks their spending. Common reasons people give:

- "I don't earn enough to budget" (backwards — you need it more)
- "It takes too much time" (modern apps take 10 minutes a week)
- "I know roughly where my money goes" (most people are wrong by 30-40%)

The truth: budgeting isn't about restriction. It's about knowing what you have so you can spend confidently on what matters.
activities.notePrompt: What is one excuse you have used (or heard) for not budgeting?

activities.title: The basics: Income vs Expenses
activities.content:
Every budget has two sides:

**Income** — money coming in:
- Wages from work
- Government support (student allowance, Working for Families)
- Interest on savings
- Other sources

**Expenses** — money going out:
- Rent, food, power, phone
- Entertainment, clothing, transport
- Savings and investments (yes, these count as expenses in a budget!)

If income > expenses = **surplus** (good!)
If expenses > income = **deficit** (danger!)
activities.notePrompt: List two of your personal income sources and two regular expenses.

questions.question: Tama earns $520/week and spends $490/week. What is his budget situation?
questions.explanation: Surplus = Income minus Expenses = $520 - $490 = $30 surplus per week. This is positive — Tama has money left over.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason why budgeting is important?
options: It shows you where your money goes
It helps you save towards goals
questions.explanation: Budgeting cannot prevent unexpected expenses — emergencies happen. But it CAN help you build an emergency fund to handle them when they do.

activities.title: Zero-based budgeting
activities.content:
One popular method is **zero-based budgeting**: every dollar of income is assigned a job until you reach zero unallocated dollars.

Example:
- Income: $600/week
- Rent: -$180
- Food: -$100
- Transport: -$40
- Phone: -$30
- Entertainment: -$50
- Savings: -$100
- Emergency fund: -$50
- Clothing: -$50
= $0 remaining

Every dollar has a purpose. Nothing is 'floating' where it gets spent without intention.
activities.notePrompt: Could you apply zero-based budgeting to your current income? What categories would you include?

questions.statement: A budget is only useful if you earn a lot of money.
questions.explanation: Budgets are actually MORE important when money is tight, not less. They help you make every dollar count.

questions.statement: Savings should be included as a line item in your budget.
questions.explanation: Treating savings as an expense that must be 'paid' each week — ideally before you spend on anything else — is key to building wealth.

questions.statement: A budget deficit means you are spending more than you earn.
questions.explanation: A deficit means outgoings exceed income. Sustained deficits lead to debt accumulation.

activities.title: Building your first budget
activities.content:
Steps to create a budget:

1. **Track your income** — list every dollar coming in weekly/monthly
2. **List fixed expenses** — rent, phone plan, subscriptions (same every month)
3. **Estimate variable expenses** — food, fuel, entertainment (vary week to week)
4. **Add savings goals** — emergency fund, holiday, item you want
5. **Check the balance** — if you have a deficit, find what to cut
6. **Review weekly** — compare what you planned vs what actually happened

Most people find their first budget is an eye-opener — especially on food and entertainment spending.
activities.notePrompt: What do you think would be your biggest budget category after rent? How much do you think you spend on it per week?

activities.instruction: Sort these items into Income or Expense

activities.title: Lesson summary
activities.content:
**Key points from this lesson:**

- A budget is a plan that allocates your income to expenses, savings and goals
- Income minus expenses = surplus (positive) or deficit (negative)
- Zero-based budgeting assigns every dollar a purpose
- Budgets work for everyone, regardless of income level
- Savings should be treated as a non-negotiable expense

In the next lesson, you will learn the 50/30/20 rule — a simple framework used by millions of people worldwide.
activities.notePrompt: What is one thing you want to change about how you currently manage money?
